body_txt: Fiction writers seem to not just confuse Mexico and Spain , as well as other Spanish-speaking countries , but to fuse them into a strange amalgam of the most general stereotypes of both, much as Scotireland fuses Scotland and Ireland. Maybe it's because they share a language and their majority religion , the fact that Mexico used to be part of the Spanish Empire , that both populations tend to be tanned and are genetically related after centuries of migration and intermarrying, that they both have exotic foods and customs compared to an Anglo-Saxon culture, note And compared to each other, as well. or simply that the author didn't check the facts and hasn't travelled much, either. American writers (and particularly those in California ) also have the excuse that Mexico is literally over the border from the US and Mexicans are the most common foreignborn population in the country (indeed, much of the western US used to be part of Mexico) while Spain is an ocean away, so the more familiar Mexican culture colors their perception of Spain. Naturally, this approach requires the writer to ignore that Mexican culture owes as much to the native cultures that existed there before the Spanish conquest, such as Maya and the Aztec, as it does to Spain's (although Hollywood has never showed its strength when having to keep those apart either ), that Mexico is more influenced by modern U.S. culture than Spain is, and that the two countries are, simply put, an ocean apart from each other and have been not under the same flag for over two centuries now, meaning that they have had ample room to develop independently from one another - be it in law, politics, holidays, food, dress, music and even language. Indeed, not only do they speak different dialects of Spanish in Spain and Mexico (the epic wars between supporters of Castilian and Latin American dubs on YouTube are a testament to that) but there are also different accents and dialects within the countries themselves. Neither does it help that Spanish immigration from Spain to the United States has been, for the most part, negligible, and if not, certainly forgotten, especially when compared to the much larger and more visible Latin American immigration numbers—though it does happen of course, with the small Spanish communities in the US preserving and taking pride in their specifically-Iberian cultures as well as any migrant group. In short, saying Spain and Mexico are the same is like saying that the United Kingdom and the United States are the same. This trope came to be due to the "Good Neighbor Policy" in the 1930s and 1940s, when America sought friendly non-interventional diplomatic ties with Latin American countries. Prior to this, works that revolve around Spanish language and culture focused directly towards Spain. Following the Spanish Civil War and Francisco Franco 's regime , media, especially Hollywood media, sought for easier and more practical alternatives, and with Franklin D. Roosevelt 's implementation of the Good Neighbor Policy, they looked no further than Mexico as a shorthand for Spain, and it stuck ever since even after World War II and the death of Franco in 1975. It took a further step in development, along with its sister trope Latin Land , with the increasing migration of non-white Hispanics from Puerto Rico, Dominican Republic, southern Mexico and Central America in The '70s and The '80s , which brought the introduction of the "Hispanic" category to the US census in 1981, and the equally increasing presence of the Caribbean in the US news cycle as a result of Ronald Reagan 's interventions in the region and The War on Drugs . Before this, Hispanics were popularly perceived in the US as white, even by authors who were aware of the existence of indigenous, black, and mixed people in Latin America. Only Cubans would retain this image in political discourse until the Donald Trump Administration, because of overhelmingly white Cuban migration to the United States before, and after the Revolution. In its simplest form, this trope is represented by a group or town that is full of stereotypically Mexican or Spanish people, set in a location or doing an activity better suited to the other. That is, when they aren't just made into a mish-mash. It could be a Spanish mariachi band at a wedding instead of a tuna singing Clavelitos , or a town of thick-mustachioed men in sombreros and ponchos dancing Flamenco. When Latin Land is brought into the mix, it can even end with Spain being depicted as a hot, tropical jungle or desert full of revolutionary outlaws, sometimes fighting a Banana Republic run by a Fascist dictator (which might have been technically true during Franco's dictatorship, except there are no tropical jungles in Spain — but it's definitely false in anything set after 1978, and that's being generous). It also takes the form of Spanish characters sporting names or surnames that are Hispanic in origin but geographically uncharacteristic. For example , Salazar, Cortez, or Chávez are common in the Americas, but actually very rare in Spain. Others may be widespread in American countries but more common in only one region of Spain, like the Basque Uribe or Andalusian Vargas. Finally, Latin Americans can also have Native American names, or originating in other countries with high historical migration to Latin America like Portugal, Italy, Germany, Eastern Europe, the Middle East, or France, which adds a new layer to consider when writing a Famous-Named Foreigner . US productions are likelier to misrepresent Spaniards more often than Mexicans, since Mexicans have many more demonstrable stereotypes in American pop-culture than Spaniards do, and they will likely have a much easier time casting Mexican actors (or from elsewhere in Latin America and the US) than Spanish ones, accents and even race be damned. The reverse also happens, with some Spanish actors having made entire careers out of playing Mexican or other Hispanic American characters in foreign cinema. In Japan, where both nations are equally exotic, the mix and mash is likelier to happen both ways, while in Europe, British productions in particular see Mexico (and Latin America outside Brazil) as little more than a region of Spain with sombreros and chili where the familiar Castilian (European) version of Spanish is spoken. See Toros y Flamenco and South of the Border for Hollywood Atlas versions of Spain and Mexico, respectively. Contrast Latin Land for a similar fusion of different countries south of the United States in a process not that different of Spexico , with only jungle or llamas added for flavor depending on the circumstances, and narcoterrorists if convenient. Compare Latino Is Brown , when fictional depictions of Latin American characters are portrayed as Mestizos. Often linked to Developing Nations Lack Cities . Compare Far East , Ancient Grome , Scotireland , Mayincatec , Ruritania , Qurac , Bulungi , and Mystical India . Spexico is not the only example of transatlantic fusion: a similar phenomenon occurs with depictions of Quebec in Hollywood movies as being full of Frenchmen with Parisian accents and mannerisms (or the other way around, casting Quebec actors in French roles despite their accent and some of their vocabulary clearly not being European French). Likewise, US productions will often use Brazilian accents and music in Portuguese settings or cast Portuguese actors as Brazilian, and outside the Anglosphere some people can't see the difference between the UK and the US either.
